title: Predrafting roving before spinning
subtitle: When and how to open up commercial roving before it meets the wheel or spindle
excerpt: Commercial roving is often more tightly compressed than the spinning wheel or spindle wants. Predrafting opens the fibre structure, reveals the staple length, and allows colour blending before a single inch of yarn is spun. The technique takes five minutes and makes the rest of the session easier.
sourceNotes: Observation-led. Predrafting is a standard preparation step taught across modern spinning guilds and is covered in Judith MacKenzie, The Intentional Spinner (referenced for substance, not quoted). The staple-length tug-test is a practical diagnostic used by hand-spinners to assess roving readiness before spinning.

glossaryTerms.definition: Drawing roving apart by hand before spinning to open the fibre structure, check the staple length, and adjust the density to a level the wheel or spindle can draft smoothly. Predrafting does not add twist; it redistributes fibre and breaks any compressed zones in the roving.

The length of an individual fibre from its root end to its tip, measured in centimetres. Knowing the staple length helps the spinner choose a drafting technique: short staples (under 8 cm) suit a woollen long-draw; long staples (12 cm and over) are easier to control with a short-forward draw that keeps both hands closer together than the staple is long.

A drafting technique in which the spinner keeps both hands close together with one hand always ahead of the drafting zone, blocking twist from running into unsupported fibre. The result is a smooth, dense yarn with fibres lying parallel. Suited to long-staple combed preparations and to spinners who want an even, hard-wearing yarn.

attrs.title: Wool dust
attrs.body: Pulling roving apart releases fine fibre dust. Work near an open window and damp-mop the surface below you after the session.

content.text: Pull a length of commercial roving off the ball and hold it between both hands, 20 cm apart. Draw your hands slowly away from each other. If the roving drafts smoothly and the fibres slide past each other without snagging, it is ready to spin. If it resists and pulls as a single dense unit, it needs
predrafting
: a deliberate loosening of the fibre structure before any twist is added.
Three situations call for predrafting. First: the roving is too compacted to draft smoothly at the wheel. Second: the
staple length
is unclear (predrafting exposes individual fibres so you can measure a few). Third: two colours of roving need to be blended by hand before spinning a heathered or gradient yarn.
What you need

items.name: Commercial roving, any breed or blend
items.qty: as much as you plan to spin in the session

items.name: Soft tape measure for checking staple length after predrafting
A clean, smooth surface (table or floor) to lay out predrafted roving

content.text: The staple-length test

Before predrafting for spinning, run a quick staple-length test on an unfamiliar roving. Tear a small tuft (about 2 g) from the roving and gently tug its two ends apart. Watch what happens: if individual fibres slip past each other smoothly, releasing without snapping, the staple is intact and the fibres are the full natural length. If the roving tears with resistance and short broken ends pull free, the roving has been over-twisted or over-processed and the fibres are already fractured. That fractured roving needs more aggressive predrafting to break up the compressed zones and release the short fibres so they can draft evenly.

To measure the actual staple length: pull a few individual fibres free from the tuft and lay them flat against a soft tape measure. Typical ranges: fine merino 4-7 cm, medium merino 7-10 cm, Corriedale 8-12 cm, Bluefaced Leicester 10-15 cm, Romney 15-20 cm. Knowing the staple length sets the hand position for your drafting technique: in a
worsted draw
, the distance between your two hands should always be less than the staple length so no fibre is unsupported. For a staple of 8 cm, keep your hands 6-7 cm apart at most.
How to predraft
Step 1: Pull off a working length

Pull 60-90 cm of roving from the ball. Do not cut it: a torn end, slightly tapered, joins onto the next length at the wheel far more smoothly than a cut end does. Set the rest of the ball to one side.
Step 2: Split the roving lengthways

Pinch the roving between the thumb and forefinger of both hands, about 10 cm apart. Pull your hands slowly apart sideways (perpendicular to the length of the roving) so the roving splits into two thinner lengths. This lengthways split is the most reliable way to reduce the roving's diameter to a size the wheel or spindle can handle. Commercial roving is often twice the thickness needed for a DK singles. Splitting it once gives the right density for most wheel spinning; split again for spindle work or very fine yarns.
Step 3: Draw it open along the length

Hold the split length between both hands, 10-15 cm apart. Draw your hands apart along the length of the roving with a slow, steady pull. Feel for resistance: smooth, even resistance means the fibres are drafting properly; a sudden catch followed by a snap means you have hit a tight compressed zone. When you feel a tight zone, stop and work it: hold the roving on either side of the tight spot, closer together (5-6 cm apart), and use a lighter, more patient pull to break it up. Work along the whole 60-90 cm length. The predrafted roving should feel noticeably lighter and more airy when finished.
Step 4: Lay out and wind loosely

Lay each predrafted length loosely on a clean surface or wind it into a loose butterfly around your fingers. Do not wind it tightly onto a ball: compression undoes the predrafting work. Predraft one or two bobbins' worth before sitting at the wheel so the spinning session is not broken by predrafting stops.
Predrafting for colour blending

To blend two colours of roving by hand, predraft both colours to the same narrow width and then hold one over the other, aligning them along the length. Draw both together as one length: the fibres from each colour will interleave in the drafting zone, producing a heathered blend in the spun yarn. For a gradient, hold the two lengths side by side and gradually replace one with the other over 20-30 cm of the predraft. The blend will be soft and organic rather than a clean break.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The roving snaps apart during predrafting instead of drafting smoothly
items.cause: Either the hands are too far apart for the staple length, or the predrafting pull is too sudden rather than steady.
items.fix: Bring your hands closer together: they should be no more than two-thirds of the staple length apart. Slow the pull right down. If the roving still snaps, it is over-processed and the fibre length is very short: treat it as a short-staple fibre and work in very small, patient increments of 3-4 cm at a time.

items.problem: The predrafted roving is uneven, with thick and thin patches
items.cause: Uneven pressure during the lengthways draw: some zones drafted too much, others too little.
items.fix: Work the thick patches again individually: hold the roving on each side of a thick zone, close your hands to 5-6 cm apart, and draft that patch specifically. One or two short passes brings it even without thinning the whole length.

items.problem: The yarn is still uneven at the wheel even after predrafting
items.cause: Predrafting improved the roving structure but the spinning draft zone is doing extra work at the remaining thick spots.
items.fix: Slow the treadle rate when approaching a thicker section of the predrafted length. The spinning draft will have less work to do if the predraft is consistent: spend more time on the predraft next session and check for compressed zones by feel before sitting at the wheel.
